plainMeaning: Hail Mahāvīra — the wellspring of all scripture, the last of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras, and the teacher of the whole world.
recitation: jayai suyāṇaṁ ppabhavo, titthayarāṇaṁ apacchimo jayai; jayai guru logāṇaṁ, jayai mahappā mahāvīro.

wordByWord.etymology: 3rd person singular present of the root √ji ('to conquer, prevail'); Sanskrit jayati. Functions as an acclamation, 'victorious is…'.
wordByWord.notes: First of four anaphoric utterances of jayai in this verse; subject throughout is Mahāvīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive plural of suya (Prakrit) < Sanskrit śruta, 'that which is heard', i.e. revealed/canonical knowledge.
Prakrit pabhavo < Sanskrit prabhava ('arising, source'); the initial p- is doubled (ppabhavo) by sandhi after the nasal of suyāṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: suyāṇaṁ ppabhavo = 'source of the scriptures', a title of Mahāvīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive plural of titthayara < Sanskrit tīrthaṅkara ('maker of the ford/crossing').
Prakrit apacchimo < Sanskrit apaścima ('that which has no other after it'; a-paścima, lit. 'not-latter' → 'last').
wordByWord.notes: titthayarāṇaṁ apacchimo = 'last of the Tīrthaṅkaras' — Mahāvīra, 24th of this avasarpiṇī.
Second jayai; closes the first half-line, repeating the acclamation.
Third jayai; opens the second line.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit/Prakrit guru ('heavy, weighty' → 'venerable teacher'); here in compound with logāṇaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: of the worlds / of all beings
wordByWord.etymology: Genitive plural of loga (Prakrit) < Sanskrit loka ('world, people, realm').

wordByWord.notes: Fourth and final jayai, leading to the named subject.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mahappā < Sanskrit mahātman (mahā + ātman, 'great-souled'); the conjunct -tm- assimilates to -pp-.
Prakrit nominative mahāvīro < Sanskrit Mahāvīra (mahā + vīra, 'great hero'); the personal name of Vardhamāna.
wordByWord.notes: The explicit subject who is finally named, gathering all four jayai onto himself.

literalTranslation: Victorious is the source of the scriptures; victorious the last of the Tīrthaṅkaras; victorious the teacher of the worlds; victorious the great-souled Mahāvīra.
idiomaticTranslation: Hail to him, the wellspring from whom all scripture flows! Hail to the last of the ford-makers! Hail to the teacher of every world! Hail to the great-souled Mahāvīra!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the opening verse hailed Mahāvīra through cosmic kinship-epithets (world-father, world-kinsman), this verse pivots to his two defining doctrinal titles and binds them with a fourfold drum-beat of jayai. The anaphora — four acclamations, each a separate 'jaya', with the named subject withheld until the very last word (mahāvīro) — builds suspense so the whole salutation lands on the name itself. The first title, suyāṇaṁ ppabhavo ('source of the scriptures'), is precisely the right key in which to open a recitation drawn from the Nandī-sūtra, the canonical treatise on knowledge: the verse credits Mahāvīra as the fountainhead of all śruta. The second, titthayarāṇaṁ apacchimo ('last of the Tīrthaṅkaras'), fixes his place as the twenty-fourth and final ford-maker of this descending age — apaścima meaning literally 'one with none after him'. The third acclamation, guru logāṇaṁ, restates in compact Prakrit the jagaguru already met in verse 1, now as a bare jaya rather than an epithet-chain. The fourth, mahappā (mahātmā), seals the praise with his greatness of soul before naming him. Thus the verse moves the hymn from 'who he is to the world' toward 'what he is to the path' — source of teaching and terminus of the Tīrthaṅkara line — and sets up the bhaddaṁ ('auspiciousness!') benediction that the following verse will pour over Vīra.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Calling Mahāvīra suyāṇaṁ pabhavo encodes the standard Jain account of how scripture arises: the Tīrthaṅkara utters meaning (artha) in his divine assembly, and the Gaṇadharas weave it into sūtra-form — so he is the origin (prabhava) of all śruta-jñāna even though he composes no text himself. This is doubly apt at the threshold of a Nandī-sūtra extract, since the Nandī is the Āgama devoted to the five kinds of knowledge (mati, śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyāya, kevala). apacchima ('last') locates him within the fixed roster of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras of this avasarpiṇī, the same roster venerated in the Logassa — Ṛṣabha first, Vardhamāna last. The verse therefore yokes two pillars of Jain reverence: the supremacy of revealed knowledge and the historical finality of Mahāvīra's tīrtha.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at the head of the Nandī sajjhāy, this verse tunes the practitioner's bhāva (inner disposition) before study: one first salutes the very source of scripture, kindling humility toward the śruta about to be recited. The simple, repeated jayai is meant to be felt, not merely parsed — a reciter can let each 'jaya' deepen reverence, arriving with full attention on the name 'Mahāvīra'.

crossReferences.text: Nandī-sūtra, opening maṅgala (praise of Mahāvīra and the five jñānas)
Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — veneration of the 24 Tīrthaṅkaras, of whom Mahāvīra is the last

sources.title: Nandi Sutra (Romanized) — Jain Quantum
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the verse as the Nandī-sūtra opening maṅgala and its praise of Mahāvīra as source of scripture and last Tīrthaṅkara

sources.title: Apaścima — Sanskrit dictionary, Wisdomlib
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and sense of apaścima/apacchima: 'having no other after it, last'

notes: The verse stacks four occurrences of jayai (all glossed); the grammatical subject is withheld until mahāvīro in the final pāda, so each 'victorious' is best read with Mahāvīra implied. ppabhavo carries a doubled initial p- by sandhi after suyāṇaṁ; some recensions print pabhavo. logāṇaṁ here functions adjectivally with guru ('teacher of the worlds'); it is the genitive plural, echoing the jagaguru of v.1. Spellings (e.g. અપચ્છિમો, મહપ્પા) follow the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book verbatim and are not normalized. The exact word-division and minor mātrā readings can vary across printed Nandī recensions; the book is treated as authoritative here.
